docs.title: AI101-IAI: Introduction to Artificial Intelligence
docs.text: Welcome to AI101, a comprehensive documentation site dedicated to exploring the world of Artificial Intelligence (AI). This project is designed as a personal and educational hub where enthusiasts, students, and professionals can learn, explore, and reference AI concepts, technologies, and applications.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Artificial Intelligence is a rapidly evolving field of computer science that focuses on creating systems capable of performing tasks that traditionally require human intelligence. From machine learning and natural language processing to computer vision and robotics, AI is transforming industries, research, and everyday life.

AI101 aims to serve as a structured, accessible, and constantly evolving repository of knowledge for anyone looking to understand AI—from foundational concepts to advanced topics.

docs.title: Features of the Documentation Site
docs.text:
• Comprehensive Coverage: Includes tutorials, guides, explanations, and real-world applications of AI across different domains.

  • Beginner-Friendly: Sections designed for newcomers to build a solid understanding of AI fundamentals.

  • Advanced Insights: In-depth discussions on modern AI techniques, algorithms, and frameworks.

  • Cross-Referencing: Easy navigation between concepts, linking related topics for a seamless learning experience.

  • Up-to-Date Resources: Curated references to papers, articles, and open-source tools to stay current with the AI landscape.

  • Hands-On Examples: Code snippets, visualizations, and practical examples to help readers experiment and learn actively.

docs.title: Key Topics
docs.text:
The AI101 site covers a wide range of AI topics, including but not limited to:

  • Foundations of AI: Introduction to AI, history, key concepts, problem-solving strategies.

  • Machine Learning: Supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning, model evaluation, and practical ML workflows.

  • Deep Learning: Neural networks, CNNs, RNNs, Transformers, and modern architectures.

  • Natural Language Processing (NLP): Text processing, language models, sentiment analysis, and question answering.

  • Computer Vision: Image recognition, object detection, video analysis, and image generation.

  • Robotics and Automation: AI in robotics, autonomous systems, and control algorithms.

  • Ethics and AI Society: Responsible AI, fairness, bias, and societal impact.

  • AI Tools & Frameworks: Overview of popular AI libraries such as TensorFlow, PyTorch, OpenCV, and more.

docs.title: Project Structure
docs.text:
AI101 is designed as a documentation site with the following structure:

AI101/
├─ docs/          # Markdown files for AI topics
├─ assets/        # Images, diagrams, and examples
├─ tutorials/     # Step-by-step tutorials and hands-on exercises
├─ examples/      # Code snippets and demo projects
├─ index.md       # Homepage / overview
└─ README.md      # Project introduction

This structure ensures content is modular, easy to maintain, and scalable as the site grows.

docs.title: Why AI101?
docs.text:
AI is a complex and fast-growing field. Many learners face the challenge of fragmented information scattered across books, papers, and online resources. AI101 consolidates essential knowledge into a single, organized, and accessible site.

Whether your goal is to learn AI from scratch, stay updated with trends, or apply AI in projects, AI101 provides the guidance, resources, and context to support your journey.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
To explore AI101:

  1. Clone the repository:

    git clone https://github.com/yourusername/AI101.git

  2. Open index.md to start reading the main content.

  3. Navigate through different topics using the sidebar or table of contents.

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text:
AI101 is an open and evolving project. Contributions are welcome! You can:

  • Add new AI topics or tutorials.

  • Improve existing content.

  • Provide code examples or visualizations.

  • Suggest edits or updates to ensure accuracy.

docs.title: License
docs.text:
This project is open-source under the MIT License. Feel free to use, modify, and share knowledge responsibly.

AI101 is more than just a documentation site—it’s a curated AI learning experience. Whether you are a student, researcher, or curious explorer, AI101 aims to be your guide in navigating the exciting world of Artificial Intelligence.

docs.title: Attachments
docs.text: • ZoranDuric-2015-OpenCV_in_Python-GMU.pdf https://cs.gmu.edu/~kosecka/cs482/code-examples/opencv-python/OpenCV_Python.pdf

docs.title: Introduction to OpenCV
docs.text:
OpenCV (Open Source Computer Vision Library) is one of the most powerful and widely used libraries for computer vision, image processing, and machine learning.

It provides tools to capture, manipulate, analyze, and understand images and videos — enabling computers to "see" and interpret the visual world.

OpenCV is written in C++, but it offers robust bindings for Python, Java, and other languages. For beginners, Python + OpenCV is the most accessible and widely used combination.

docs.title: 1. What Is OpenCV?
docs.text:
OpenCV was originally developed by Intel in 1999 to promote real-time computer vision research. Today, it’s maintained by the OpenCV Foundation and supported by a large open-source community.

Its goal is to provide a unified framework for visual computing — from simple image transformations to advanced object detection and deep learning.

docs.title: Example Capabilities
docs.text:
• Image reading, writing, and displaying
  • Color space conversions
  • Edge, contour, and feature detection
  • Object recognition and tracking
  • Face and motion detection
  • Integration with deep learning frameworks (TensorFlow, PyTorch, ONNX)

docs.title: Python Installation
docs.text:
You can install OpenCV easily with pip:

pip install opencv-python

To include advanced (non-free) features like SIFT or SURF, install:

pip install opencv-contrib-python

Verify installation:

import cv2
print(cv2.__version__)

docs.title: 3.1 Image Representation
docs.text:
In OpenCV, an image is represented as a NumPy array, where each pixel corresponds to a set of color values.

  • Grayscale image: 2D array (height × width)

  • Color image (BGR): 3D array (height × width × 3)

Example:

import cv2

img = cv2.imread('example.jpg')  # Load image
print(img.shape)  # Output: (height, width, 3)

⚠️ OpenCV uses BGR (Blue-Green-Red) order instead of RGB.

docs.title: 3.2 Reading, Displaying, and Saving Images
docs.text:
import cv2

# Read an image
img = cv2.imread('cat.jpg')

# Display it in a window
cv2.imshow('Cat', img)
cv2.waitKey(0)  # Wait for key press
cv2.destroyAllWindows()

# Save the image
cv2.imwrite('cat_copy.jpg', img)
  • cv.imread()
  • cv.imshow()
  • cv.imwrite()
  • cv.waitKey()
  • cv.destroyAllWindows()

docs.title: 3.3 Video Capture
docs.text:
You can capture video from a file or a webcam.

import cv2

cap = cv2.VideoCapture(0)  # 0 = default webcam

while True:
    ret, frame = cap.read()
    if not ret:
        break

    cv2.imshow('Camera', frame)

    if cv2.waitKey(1) == ord('q'):  # Press 'q' to quit
        break

cap.release()
cv2.destroyAllWindows()

docs.title: 4.1 Resize and Crop
docs.text:
resized = cv2.resize(img, (300, 200))
cropped = img[50:200, 100:300]

docs.title: 4.2 Color Conversion
docs.text:
gray = cv2.cvtColor(img, cv2.COLOR_BGR2GRAY)
hsv = cv2.cvtColor(img, cv2.COLOR_BGR2HSV)

docs.title: 5.1 Blurring
docs.text: blur = cv2.GaussianBlur(img, (5, 5), 0)

docs.title: 5.2 Edge Detection
docs.text: edges = cv2.Canny(img, 100, 200)

docs.title: 5.3 Thresholding
docs.text:
gray = cv2.cvtColor(img, cv2.COLOR_BGR2GRAY)
_, thresh = cv2.threshold(gray, 127, 255, cv2.THRESH_BINARY)

docs.title: 5.4 Contour Detection
docs.text:
contours, _ = cv2.findContours(thresh, cv2.RETR_TREE, cv2.CHAIN_APPROX_SIMPLE)
cv2.drawContours(img, contours, -1, (0, 255, 0), 2)

docs.title: 6.1 Translation
docs.text:
M = np.float32([[1, 0, 100], [0, 1, 50]])  # Move 100px right, 50px down
shifted = cv2.warpAffine(img, M, (img.shape[1], img.shape[0]))

docs.title: 6.2 Rotation
docs.text:
(h, w) = img.shape[:2]
center = (w // 2, h // 2)
M = cv2.getRotationMatrix2D(center, 45, 1.0)
rotated = cv2.warpAffine(img, M, (w, h))

docs.title: 6.3 Flipping
docs.text: flip_horizontal = cv2.flip(img, 1)

docs.title: 7.1 Corner Detection
docs.text:
gray = np.float32(cv2.cvtColor(img, cv2.COLOR_BGR2GRAY))
corners = cv2.goodFeaturesToTrack(gray, 25, 0.01, 10)
for corner in corners:
    x, y = corner.ravel()
    cv2.circle(img, (int(x), int(y)), 5, (0, 0, 255), -1)

docs.title: 7.2 SIFT (Scale-Invariant Feature Transform)
docs.text:
Requires opencv-contrib-python:

sift = cv2.SIFT_create()
keypoints, descriptors = sift.detectAndCompute(gray, None)
cv2.drawKeypoints(img, keypoints, img)

docs.title: 8. Face Detection
docs.text:
OpenCV includes Haar cascades for face and eye detection.

face_cascade = cv2.CascadeClassifier(cv2.data.haarcascades + 'haarcascade_frontalface_default.xml')

gray = cv2.cvtColor(img, cv2.COLOR_BGR2GRAY)
faces = face_cascade.detectMultiScale(gray, 1.3, 5)

for (x, y, w, h) in faces:
    cv2.rectangle(img, (x, y), (x+w, y+h), (255, 0, 0), 2)

docs.title: 9. Integration with Deep Learning
docs.text:
OpenCV supports importing pre-trained neural networks via DNN (Deep Neural Network) module.

You can load models from:

  • TensorFlow (.pb)

  • Caffe (.prototxt, .caffemodel)

  • ONNX (.onnx)

Example:

net = cv2.dnn.readNetFromONNX('model.onnx')
blob = cv2.dnn.blobFromImage(img, 1/255.0, (224, 224))
net.setInput(blob)
output = net.forward()

docs.title: 10. Real-World Applications
docs.text: Field Example Security Face recognition, surveillance cameras Healthcare Medical image segmentation Autonomous Vehicles Lane detection, object tracking Augmented Reality Marker tracking Robotics Machine vision for object manipulation Education Teaching visual computing concepts

docs.title: 11. Example: Real-Time Edge Detection
docs.text:
import cv2

cap = cv2.VideoCapture(0)

while True:
    ret, frame = cap.read()
    gray = cv2.cvtColor(frame, cv2.COLOR_BGR2GRAY)
    edges = cv2.Canny(gray, 50, 150)

    cv2.imshow('Edges', edges)

    if cv2.waitKey(1) == ord('q'):
        break

cap.release()
cv2.destroyAllWindows()

Press Q to quit the window.

docs.title: 12. Performance Tips
docs.text: ✅ Convert images to grayscale early when color is unnecessary ✅ Resize large images to smaller dimensions for real-time speed ✅ Use ROI (region of interest) to limit processing area ✅ Use GPU acceleration via OpenCV CUDA (if available) ✅ Pre-load models to avoid re-initialization cost

docs.title: 13. Integrating OpenCV with Other Tools
docs.text:
• NumPy: for numerical operations on image arrays

  • Matplotlib: for visualizing results in notebooks

  • TensorFlow / PyTorch: for advanced neural networks

  • Flask / FastAPI: for building image-processing web APIs

  • Raspberry Pi: for embedded vision projects

docs.title: 14. OpenCV Ecosystem
docs.text:
• opencv-python → Core image/video operations

  • opencv-contrib-python → Extra modules (SIFT, SURF, tracking, etc.)

  • cvzone → Simplified wrapper for common tasks

  • mediapipe → Google’s library often used with OpenCV for pose or hand detection

docs.title: 15. Conclusion
docs.text:
OpenCV bridges the gap between raw image data and intelligent computer vision applications. It provides an efficient, open-source foundation for learning and building image processing, machine learning, and AI-based visual systems.

Whether you’re exploring basic image filters or implementing face detection, OpenCV is a must-have toolkit for anyone studying or practicing computer vision.

Further Reading

  • Official OpenCV Documentation

  • OpenCV-Python Tutorials

  • OpenCV GitHub Repository

  • PyImageSearch Blog

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
OpenCV (Open Source Computer Vision Library) is an open-source computer vision and image-processing library originally developed by Intel. It provides a comprehensive set of tools for real-time image processing, computer vision, and machine learning tasks.

OpenCV supports multiple languages — including Python, C++, and Java — and runs on various platforms such as Windows, macOS, Linux, and even mobile devices.

This document introduces OpenCV’s most commonly used functions and workflows to help you quickly get started with practical image and video processing tasks.

docs.title: 1. Installation and Import *
docs.text:
To install OpenCV for Python:

pip install opencv-python
pip install opencv-contrib-python

Import the library in your Python code:

import cv2

docs.title: 2. Reading and Displaying Images *
docs.text:
OpenCV uses the cv2.imread() and cv2.imshow() functions to read and display images.

import cv2

# Read an image
img = cv2.imread('example.jpg')

# Display the image in a window
cv2.imshow('Display Window', img)

# Wait for a key press
cv2.waitKey(0)
cv2.destroyAllWindows()

💡 cv2.waitKey(0) means the program will wait indefinitely for a key press before closing the window.

docs.title: 3. Image Properties and Basic Info *
docs.text:
You can easily access image dimensions, type, and pixel values.

print(img.shape)   # (height, width, channels)
print(img.size)    # Total number of pixels
print(img.dtype)   # Data type, e.g., uint8

To access individual pixel values:

px = img[100, 50]  # Get BGR value at (x=50, y=100)
print(px)

To modify pixels:

img[100, 50] = [255, 255, 255]  # Set to white

docs.title: 4. Image Color Conversions
docs.text:
Color space conversion is fundamental in image processing.

gray = cv2.cvtColor(img, cv2.COLOR_BGR2GRAY)
hsv = cv2.cvtColor(img, cv2.COLOR_BGR2HSV)

Common conversions:

  • cv2.COLOR_BGR2GRAY — Convert to grayscale = 0

  • cv2.COLOR_BGR2RGB — Convert from BGR to RGB

  • cv2.COLOR_BGR2HSV — Convert to HSV color space

docs.title: Resizing:
docs.text: resized = cv2.resize(img, (400, 300))

docs.title: Cropping (via NumPy slicing):
docs.text: cropped = img[50:200, 100:400]

docs.title: 6. Drawing Shapes and Text
docs.text:
OpenCV allows drawing geometric shapes and adding text to images.

cv2.line(img, (0, 0), (200, 200), (255, 0, 0), 3)
cv2.rectangle(img, (50, 50), (250, 150), (0, 255, 0), 2)
cv2.circle(img, (150, 100), 50, (0, 0, 255), -1)
cv2.putText(img, 'OpenCV', (50, 250), cv2.FONT_HERSHEY_SIMPLEX, 1, (255, 255, 255), 2)

docs.title: 7. Image Thresholding
docs.text:
Thresholding separates objects from the background by converting grayscale images into binary images.

gray = cv2.cvtColor(img, cv2.COLOR_BGR2GRAY)
_, thresh = cv2.threshold(gray, 127, 255, cv2.THRESH_BINARY)

Common types:

  • THRESH_BINARY

  • THRESH_BINARY_INV

  • THRESH_TRUNC

  • THRESH_TOZERO

docs.title: Using Canny Edge Detector:
docs.text:
edges = cv2.Canny(img, 100, 200)

This detects edges based on gradient changes and thresholds.

docs.title: 9. Image Blurring and Filtering
docs.text:
Blurring helps remove noise and smooth images.

blur = cv2.GaussianBlur(img, (5, 5), 0)
median = cv2.medianBlur(img, 5)
bilateral = cv2.bilateralFilter(img, 9, 75, 75)

docs.title: 10. Contour Detection
docs.text:
Contours are useful for shape analysis and object detection.

gray = cv2.cvtColor(img, cv2.COLOR_BGR2GRAY)
_, thresh = cv2.threshold(gray, 127, 255, cv2.THRESH_BINARY)
contours, _ = cv2.findContours(thresh, cv2.RETR_EXTERNAL, cv2.CHAIN_APPROX_SIMPLE)

cv2.drawContours(img, contours, -1, (0, 255, 0), 2)

docs.title: Reading from a file or camera:
docs.text:
cap = cv2.VideoCapture('video.mp4')  # or 0 for webcam

while True:
    ret, frame = cap.read()
    if not ret:
        break

    cv2.imshow('Video', frame)
    if cv2.waitKey(1) & 0xFF == ord('q'):
        break

cap.release()
cv2.destroyAllWindows()

docs.title: Writing a video:
docs.text: out = cv2.VideoWriter('output.mp4', cv2.VideoWriter_fourcc(*'mp4v'), 20, (640, 480))

docs.title: 12. Face Detection (Using Haar Cascades)
docs.text:
OpenCV includes pretrained classifiers for face detection.

face_cascade = cv2.CascadeClassifier(cv2.data.haarcascades + 'haarcascade_frontalface_default.xml')

gray = cv2.cvtColor(img, cv2.COLOR_BGR2GRAY)
faces = face_cascade.detectMultiScale(gray, 1.1, 4)

for (x, y, w, h) in faces:
    cv2.rectangle(img, (x, y), (x+w, y+h), (255, 0, 0), 2)

docs.title: 13. Saving Images
docs.text: cv2.imwrite('output.jpg', img)

docs.title: 14. Common Tips
docs.text:
• OpenCV uses BGR color order, not RGB. Always convert if mixing with libraries like matplotlib.

  • Use NumPy operations directly on images for speed.

  • Always call cv2.destroyAllWindows() after displaying windows to release memory.

docs.title: 15. Summary
docs.text:
OpenCV provides a powerful, flexible, and efficient toolkit for:

  • Image reading, manipulation, and filtering

  • Object detection and tracking

  • Video processing

  • Real-time vision and machine learning

Mastering the common APIs listed here gives you a strong foundation for more advanced topics like deep learning-based computer vision, AR, or autonomous systems.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• OpenCV Official Documentation

  • OpenCV GitHub Repository

  • OpenCV Python Tutorials

docs.title: Read an Image
docs.text: This example shows how to read and display an image using OpenCV.

docs.title: Code Example
docs.text:
import cv2

# Read an image from file
img = cv2.imread('assets/images/cat.jpg')

# Display the image
cv2.imshow('Cat', img)
cv2.waitKey(0)
cv2.destroyAllWindows()

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• cv2.imread(path) loads the image as a NumPy array.
  • Use cv2.IMREAD_GRAYSCALE to load as grayscale.
  • Remember to call cv2.waitKey(0); otherwise, the window will close immediately.

docs.title: Read and Display a Video File
docs.text: This example demonstrates how to read and display a video file frame by frame using OpenCV. It can also be adapted to display a live webcam feed by changing the video source.

docs.title: Code Example
docs.text:
"""
-------------------------------------------------------
Video File Reading and Display using OpenCV
-------------------------------------------------------
"""

import cv2

# Open the video file (replace with 0 for webcam)
video_capture = cv2.VideoCapture('video.mp4')

# Check if the video file was opened successfully
if not video_capture.isOpened():
    print("Error: Cannot open the video file.")
    exit()

# Continuously read frames from the video
while True:
    ret, frame = video_capture.read()

    # If reading fails (end of video), break the loop
    if not ret:
        print("End of video or failed to read frame.")
        break

    # Convert the frame from BGR to grayscale (optional)
    frame_gray = cv2.cvtColor(frame, cv2.COLOR_BGR2GRAY)

    # Display the current frame
    cv2.imshow('Video', frame_gray)

    # Wait for 60 ms and check if the 'Esc' key (27) is pressed
    if cv2.waitKey(60) == 27:
        print("Video playback interrupted by user.")
        break

# Release resources
video_capture.release()
cv2.destroyAllWindows()

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
• cv2.VideoCapture('video.mp4') creates a video capture object to read the file. Use cv2.VideoCapture(0) to open the default webcam.
  • isOpened() checks whether the file or device was successfully opened.
  • The while loop continuously reads frames using .read().
    • ret indicates whether the frame was successfully read.
    • frame is the actual image array.
  • cv2.cvtColor(frame, cv2.COLOR_BGR2GRAY) converts the frame to grayscale. This step is optional but often useful for processing.
  • cv2.imshow() displays the frame in a window.
  • cv2.waitKey(60) waits for 60 ms between frames; pressing Esc (key code 27) stops playback.
  • release() and destroyAllWindows() properly close resources.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Make sure the video file (video.mp4) is in the same directory as your script, or provide an absolute path.
  • To adjust playback speed, change the cv2.waitKey() delay (smaller = faster).
  • If you only see a blank window, check whether the path to the video is correct.
  • You can extend this script to record or process frames in real time.
